name: The Vereda from the El Vado reservoir — circular through the Hoz de Vallosera (PR-GU 18)
name_original: La Vereda desde el embalse de El Vado —  circular por la Hoz de Vallosera (PR-GU 18)
region_intro: Bus station hiking trails around Embalse de El Vado are situated within the Sierra Norte de Guadalajara, a region characterized by mountainous terrain and dense forests. The landscape features a mix of pine, oak, and holm oak woodlands, alongside dramatic slate cliffs. The reservoir itself offers tranquil waters framed by these mountains, with views towards Pico Ocejón. This area forms part of the Parque Natural de la Sierra Norte de Guadalajara, known for its "Black Architecture" villages. Best bus station hiking trails around Embalse de El Vado The most popular bus station hiking route is Retiendas Village loop from Tamajón, a 6.5 miles (10.4 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 52 minutes to complete. This route explores the local villages and surrounding natural environment. Another top favourite among local hikers is Almiruete loop from Tamajón, a moderate 7.7 miles (12.3 km) path. It offers varied terrain through the natural park, connecting Tamajón with the village of Almiruete. Local hikers also love the Bonaval Monastery Ruins – Retiendas Village loop from Tamajón, an 8.5 miles (13.7 km) trail leading through forests and past historical ruins, often completed in about 3 hours 45 minutes. Hiking around Embalse de El Vado is defined by dense pine forests, oak woodlands, and views of the reservoir and surrounding mountains. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging moderate routes. The routes in Embalse de El Vado are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 900 hikers have used komoot to explore Embalse de El Vado's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many bus-accessible hiking trails are available around Embalse de El Vado?
A: There are 5 hiking trails around Embalse de El Vado that are easily accessible from bus stations, primarily in the village of Tamajón. These routes offer a range of experiences, from easy strolls to more moderate excursions through the natural landscape.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect on these bus-accessible trails?
A: The trails accessible by bus around Embalse de El Vado traverse a diverse landscape. You'll encounter dense pine, oak, and holm oak forests, dramatic slate cliffs, and picturesque views of the reservoir's tranquil turquoise waters, often framed by the distant Pico Ocejón. The region is part of the Sierra Norte de Guadalajara, known for its rugged beauty.

Q: Are there any easy hiking options from a bus station for beginners or those seeking a relaxed walk?
A: Yes, for an easier experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38222160" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Enchanted City of Tamajón – Climbing area loop from Tamajón&lt;/a&gt;. This route is approximately 4.8 kilometers long with minimal elevation gain, making it perfect for beginners or a leisurely outing. It starts directly from Tamajón, a key bus stop in the area.

Q: What are the best times of year to hike around Embalse de El Vado using public transport?
A: Late spring, early summer, and after autumn rains are generally ideal for hiking around Embalse de El Vado. During these periods, the water levels are often beautiful, and the natural scenery is at its most vibrant. The region's diverse flora and fauna are also more active, offering a richer experience.

Q: Are there any circular routes accessible from bus stations?
A: Yes, all the bus-accessible routes in this guide are circular, meaning you'll end your hike back at your starting point, typically the bus station in Tamajón. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14885798" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Retiendas Village loop from Tamajón&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31511696" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hiking loop from Tamajón&lt;/a&gt;, offering convenient round trips.

Q: What historical or cultural points of interest can I see on these trails?
A: The region is rich in history. Many trails offer glimpses of the distinctive "Black Architecture" villages, built with dark slate. While not directly on every bus-accessible route, the area is close to the evocative ruins of the Church of Nuestra Señora de la Blanca, part of the submerged village of El Vado, which can sometimes be seen when water levels are low. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14885827" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bonaval Monastery Ruins – Retiendas Village loop from Tamajón&lt;/a&gt; specifically includes historical ruins.

Q: Are the bus-accessible trails around Embalse de El Vado suitable for families?
A: Yes, the area offers options suitable for families. The easier routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38222160" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Enchanted City of Tamajón – Climbing area loop from Tamajón&lt;/a&gt;, are great for families with children due to their shorter distance and gentle terrain. The natural beauty and potential for wildlife spotting can also be very engaging for younger hikers.

Q: What wildlife might I encounter while hiking near Embalse de El Vado?
A: The Embalse de El Vado area is home to diverse wildlife. Keep an eye out for griffon vultures soaring above the cliffs, eagles, and peregrine falcons. Near the water's edge, you might spot kingfishers, and in the forests, deer are occasionally seen. The tranquil environment provides excellent opportunities for nature observation.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the bus-accessible trails in Embalse de El Vado?
A: The komoot community highly rates the hiking experience around Embalse de El Vado, with an average score of 4.77 stars. Reviewers often praise the stunning natural landscapes, the tranquility of the forests, and the picturesque views of the reservoir. The convenience of starting directly from bus stations in villages like Tamajón is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging moderate routes accessible by bus?
A: Absolutely. For those looking for a longer, moderate challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31512048" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almiruete loop from Tamajón&lt;/a&gt; offer distances of over 12 kilometers with moderate elevation changes. These trails provide a more immersive experience in the region's forests and offer expansive views.
tips: Views of the El Vado reservoir in the middle of the path that runs through the Royuelo ravine.

Uninhabited village located in the municipality of Campillo de Ranas (Guadalajara, Spain). It is located in the Sierra de Ayllón.

Historically it was part of the council of El Vado, which was made up of the villages of La Vereda, Matallana and the town of El Vado itself.
On the banks of Arroyo Vallosera, under the extraordinarily pretty town of La Vereda, it is a place to rest from the path traveled on the road or in life.

waypoints.name: Views of the reservoir from the Royuelo ravine
